methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $63M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: 1X Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $10B

private.name: Bedrock Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.75B

private.name: Dexterity
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.65B

private.name: Collaborative Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M+

private.name: Fairmat
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $350M

private.name: GrayMatter Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $300M+

data.narrative:
Standard Bots is a Series B robotics and manufacturing company founded by Evan Beard and David Golden that has raised $63M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 9 private companies and 1 public company operating in the same sector, with particular focus on a peer set of 6 Series B-stage robotics firms including 1X Technologies, Bedrock Robotics, Dexterity, Collaborative Robotics, and Fairmat.

Two valuation methods were applied. The stage-progression approach uses the company's $63M total raised as a proxy baseline and applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement, yielding an implied valuation of $120M. The peer-median method identifies six directly comparable Series B robotics companies with a median valuation of $1.1B, though the peer set exhibits substantial dispersion ranging from $300M to $10.0B, reflecting heterogeneity in market positioning and stage interpretation within the cohort.

The triangulated analysis across both methods supports a valuation range of $102M (low) to $1.2B (high), with a median of $597M. The substantial gap between stage-progression and peer-median results reflects uncertainty around whether Standard Bots should be valued toward the smaller end of the Series B robotics spectrum or closer to established frontier peers. Key uncertainties include the durability of the peer median given the wide range, capital intensity of the robotics vertical, and near-term revenue trajectory relative to comparables.
